item.title: Blog: 40 words
item.description:
无意间看到两句古文，就一时兴起，上网搜索了论语、颜家家训等，一下子竟凑成了十句四十词，真是好一番自娱自乐 :)

慈而不悲
述而不辩

念而不贪
进而不傲

谦而不虚
沉而不怠

勇而不敢
强而不势

施而不奢
俭而不啬

item.title: Blog: Time
item.description:
What are some ways to spend some time with yourself?

  • Watch Minecraft with RTX Beta | Official Worlds Tour
  • Watch smiling animals
  • Get lost, literally

item.title: Blog: A laugh a day
item.description:
What are some positive reinforcement systems that you know?

I have created a very successful positive reinforcement system — the cooking-compliment system. By giving tons of compliments to my mom whenever she cooks, I have successfully helped her to become a good chef, although I’m not sure whether she realized it. But, yeah, you are very welcome, mom! (However, obviously, the system didn’t work for everyone, e.g. my dad. Sorry, dad, I just can’t do it ;) )


Recently, I realized I also haven’t said thank you to some people who have built a positive reinforcement system around me. So here, I would like to write down a list of people’s names and say ‘thank you’. Thank you very much for helping me, referring me, or bringing me new opportunities in the past year. I would be more than happy to do the same to you and your friends in the future. But, emh, I just hope I can still figure out whether you are one of the people represented by these letters after one week ;)
BJ BP CA CU DC
DD DL FA GS GT
HO JO JT LN LS
MD MN MS NR PN
RF SL TS


Image from fsm-media

item.title: Blog: Science of Laughter
item.description:
What is the science of laughter?

Imagination.


Because, on one hand, a person who is good at creating an imaginative context can often make good jokes. On the other hand, I find people who can easily laugh are the ones who like to imagine/picture the context.

If my scientific finding can be confirmed, it means we can use the LOL threshold as a negative correlation coefficient of the imaginative ability. So, what is your LOL threshold (within the range of 0 to 10)?

If your threshold is 0 (meaning you laugh all the time?!), then I wonder how much alcohol you have every day. If your threshold is 10, congratulations, you have a bright future in doing commercials of anti-wrinkle cream ;)



From Garfield comic


Read more

  • Can imagination be measured?

  • A New Measure of Imagination Ability: Anatomical Brain Imaging Correlates

item.title: Blog: Farmers and Climate
item.description:
What would you suggest farmers growing as the climate warms?

I would suggest farmers growing hot spices, such as chilli and Si Chuan pepper.

Because eating spicy food can stimulate sweating and help people feel cooler. Therefore, I boldly predict the spicy cuisine will just get more and more popular as global warming continues. One evidence I have seen is the exponential growth of Si Chuan restaurants in China over the last N years.

Wait a minute, did I just introduce a new index of global warming, the chilli (production/consumption) index? Great, I’m gonna name it Chillindex!


Image from Red House Spice


Read more about why spicy food keeps you cooler

  • Spicy foods excite the receptors in the skin that normally respond to heat. The central nervous system reacts to whatever the sensory system tells it is going on. Therefore, the pattern of activity from pain and warm nerve fibers triggers both the sensations and the physical reactions of heat, including vasodilation, sweating, and flushing. Source

  • Hot stuff – do people living in hot climates like their food spicy hot or not?

item.title: Blog: A Smart Girl
item.description:
Was there a moment you realised you were not as smart as you thought?

I had countless moments ;) But the first time I realised this was when I was 6 years old. We had an apple peeling competition in our class. I swotted up on apple peeling skills with the help of my dad. Then I confidently went to school with a ‘sharp and special’ knife I found in the kitchen.


After the competition started, it didn’t take me too long to realise the knife was totally different from the one I knew how to use (I know, right? I was that smart). As a result, the apple in my hands was not different from one eaten by a mouse. The only thing looked like an apple was probably my red face.


However, I saw the camera man was walking towards my seat. I thought maybe I was still performing better than many of my classmates, as I always felt in exams. So I squeezed a big smile to the camera man and maintained the smile for some minutes until I realised I was not his aim. He was targeting my deskmate, Liu, a boy who was not so good in math. At that moment, I got hundreds of question marks in my head. Why?


But when I saw the long, smooth, and beautiful apple peels flowing from his simple knife, I knew the KongFu legend stories were true: the extraordinary masters always disguise themselves as ordinary people (or students?!) and only show their skills when necessary. From this day on, I learned not to judge people and stopped being an arrogant kid.


Later, my parents invited Liu over for dinner to thank him for the story that made their day (…I know, right?!). They also started helping him with math (as his grandparents were obviously overqualified to teach him). Liu then became my new superhero for quite a while, because he helped me to achieve my long-lasting wish — reducing my parents’ attention on me. What an awesome deskmate I had ;)


Image from SBS

item.title: Blog: Good Bye
item.description:
Is there anything good out of goodbye?

Hmm, a chance of receiving presents?

item.title: Blog: Human vs. Machine
item.description:
What are some some tasks human and machine can perform equally bad?

Detection of sarcasm

Where to get data?

  • Twitter: use hashtag #humor as label

Are there tools to detect sarcasm?

  • Yes?! Check out torchMoji from huggingface

Read more

  • Can an algorithm detect sarcasm Identifying Sarcasm in Twitter: A Closer Look

  • From humor recognition to irony detection: The figurative language of social media

  • Detecting Sarcasm with Deep Convolutional Neural Networks

item.title: Blog: Programming
item.description:
What is a thing that you like? And why?

Programming.

One one hand, it’s like a good game. It’s hard to level up but can always satisfy you to keep you playing.

On the other hand, it’s also like an addictive game. It can easily take up your time and make you think about it, even when you are having a dinner or a vacation. Btw, is this what being parents feel like? ;)

In summary, programming is a thing you should be cautious with and the above are some of the weirdest words I have ever said.

item.title: Blog: A Magic
item.description:
Do you have a magic that makes your goals come true?

Mine is a sentence ‘Never have I ever had X’. As long as I say the sentence, I can almost ensure I will have X come true. However, I was too naive to realise the hidden opportunities. The X I said were all things like ‘lost a wallet’ or ‘cried watching a movie’.

But now since I found my psychic power, I would need to be more strategic and use my potential to achieve some meaningful goals. So from today, I better start repeating my magic sentence with X == a GPU or X == the cheese grater ;)

Image from Imgur

item.title: Blog: A Truth
item.description:
What is a truth that you learned by yourself when you were young?


Mine is “Don’t fight the cat! (The real cat)”

item.title: Blog: Hackathons
item.description:
Why couldn’t I stop going to hackathons?


From Feb 2018 to May 2019, I have joined five hackathons on five topics. I’m very grateful to the people who “dragged” me into hackathons. I’m also thankful to my good luck that always made me surrounded by good teammates ;)

Every hackathon started with the same questions: Is this the right topic for me? Is it a waste of my time? Every hackathon also ended with similar answers: I learned something about this new topic. I met some people I can really learn a lot from.

I hope, next time, I would stop asking myself the Right/Wrong question. Life is short. Why do I need to spend time putting unnecessary principles on my interests? It might be my weakness that I do not align all my energy with one topic. But, at least, I won’t regret that I missed a chance to explore the field that I’m curious about. One day, if I stop going to a hackathon, it’s probably because the coffee there is not good, lol.

item.title: Blog: A Knight of the Seven Kingdoms
item.description:
GoT S8E2

Game of Thrones (GoT) is still running. But I think I have already got my favourite episode. I wish the the time would stop there — the moment of safety and rest, and the night of self-awareness and mutual-encouragement.

For a GoT fan, it has been such an inspiring journey to see how the POV characters have become a different version of themselves.

I guess this is a good thing regarding heart growing. But if they could, I’m curious which version of themselves they would choose? Would they choose to live like a golden lion or a Stark solider, a greenhouse flower or a winter tree?

Lol, I guess they have no choice. Only he has ;)


George RR Marten, image from lzismile

item.title: Blog: Happy Fools’ Day
item.description:
Why do I like Fools’ Day too?


As a millennial, I have got used to celebrating so many festivals, modern, traditional, western, eastern, festivals on Gregorian calendar, and festivals on Lunar calendar. Honestly, how did I manage to remember all of these festivals in the days without my smart phone, especially the ones not associated with food?

Among all the festivals, April Fools’ Day was one of my favorites during school time, because it gave me an official opportunity to explore my talents. When I look back today, I really admire my family and friends’ tolerance ;)

But besides showing off my creating-prank talents, are there other reasons why I like the Fools’ Day?

Like most of the Chinese kids, I grew up with reciting traditional poetry, from Confucius to Mencius, from Three-Character Canon to Hundred Family Surnames，and from Li Bai to Du Fu. I’m happy that I learned a lot of our ancients’ wisdom during play/breakfast time and can still communicate with my talented niblings today. And I guess this immersion mechanism is probably a way to preserve the ancient Chinese language, as well as helping kids learn right from wrong.

But one thing our ancients did not teach us well in thousands of years is how to unplug and unwind (Maybe except Philosophers? I need to find a Day to talk about them). However, we have learned this from the western culture in only 40 years. And I can really see that the Chinese culture is transitioning from a restrained culture to a more expressive culture by valuing and expressing (high arousal) emotions on various occasions, such as Mother’s Day, Father’s Day, and Fools’ Day (Emh, this sequence is strange). By continuing expressing love or making pranks on these occasions, I wish to bring more laughter to people I care and build deeper connections with them.

Now, let me think, what pranks will I make/receive today?


Is there a rule of thumb of making pranks?

My rule is to remember my goal — bringing people fun instead of testing people.

What foolish things have I done?

I probably need to find 300 Days to write them down. So I better forget them.

And what foolish things have you done?


Three ancient Chinese poems

“Man on earth, good at birth. The same nature，varies on nurture.” — Three-Character Canon
Translation source


“Neither riches nor honors can corrupt him; neither poverty nor humbleness can make him swerve from principle; and neither threats nor forces can subdue him. These characteristics constitute the great man.” — Mencius
Translation source


Thoughts in the Silent Night
— Li Bai

“Beside my bed is a pool of moonlight

Or can it be hoarfrost on the ground?

Looking up, I see the moon,

Bowing, I think of home.”
Translation adapted

item.title: Blog: Quantum Computing
item.description:
What is Quantum Computing?

  • Quantum computing is the use of quantum-mechanical phenomena such as superposition and entanglement to perform computation.

  • A quantum computer is used to perform such computation, which can be implemented theoretically or physically.



1. How is Quantum Computing different from regular computing?

Quantum Computing
Bit identity A more fluid and nonbinary spectrum
Uniqueness Superposition remains intact
Advantage Faster computation
Reality Fluid Quantum
Application Quantum encryption key, Quantum network, medicine, & teleportation (!)


2. Reference

Quantum computing explained in 10 minutes, by Shohini Ghose



3. Watch More

  • If You Don’t Understand Quantum Physics, Try This!

  • Quantum Mechanics Trypophobia alert



4. Rabbit Holes

  • Superposition/: the adding together (Superimposing) of waves

  • Entanglement: is a physical phenomenon that occurs when pairs or groups of particles are generated, interact, or share spatial proximity in ways such that the quantum state of each particle cannot be described independently of the state of the other(s), even when the particles are separated by a large distance

  • Wave function: in quantum physics, is a mathematical description of the quantum state of an isolated quantum system. The wave function is a complex-valued probability amplitude, and the probabilities for the possible results of measurements made on the system can be derived from it. The most common symbols for a wave function are the Greek letters ψ or Ψ (lower-case and capital psi, respectively).

  • Quantum Tunnelling: the quantum mechanical phenomenon where a subatomic particle passes through a potential barrier that it cannot surmount under the provision of classical mechanics.

  • Double slit problem: a demonstration that light and matter can display characteristics of both classically defined waves and particles; moreover, it displays the fundamentally probabilistic nature of quantum mechanical phenomena. The experiment was first performed with light by Thomas Young in 1801. In 1927, Davisson and Germer demonstrated that electrons show the same behavior, which was later extended to atoms and molecules.

  • Heisenberg’s uncertainty principle: the more precisely the position of some particle is determined, the less precisely its momentum can be known, and vice versa.

  • Erwin Schrödinger’s cat

  • What’s are the differences between we see/feel/hold vs Quantum world?

    • Particles vs Waves (ripples on a pond) / Wavicle
    • Certainty vs Probability (a particle may be found in many places at the same time )

  • How can Quantum computing help me to understand and solve problems?

item.title: Blog: Happiness Day
item.description:
What day is March 20?

  • Wednesday? Yes, this year. Anything else?

At home, this is my day of running to spring pancakes. Because March 20 is spring equinox, known as Chunfen in the traditional East Asian calendar. The combination of wrapped smoked meat and bean sprouts, the balance between pancake tenderness and flakiness, and the element of self-care and spring smell always make my March 20 such a great day.

Yesterday, I find another reason to like March 20, because it’s the International Day of Happiness! What a day!

This special day also made me think what are the things that make me happy in life, besides my spring pancakes and other 99 types of food. And scientifically speaking, the happiness brought by my food fellows is, in fact, a short-term pleasure. But the memories associated with food can be long-term happiness.

So how can we differentiate between pleasure and happiness? A simple way would be to ask the brain one question: is this something I want or I like? If you want to know more ways, here is a table for you ;)


What are the things making you happy? And what to do if you are unhappy?

  • Being unhappy is important. But don’t get addicted ;) Read more:

    • The Subtle Art of Not Giving a F**k: A Counterintuitive Approach to Living a Good Life, by Mark Manson
    • Unlock the benefits of your negative emotions, by Susan David

  • Being happy is a skill. Positive psychology is the science behind developing happiness skills. Read more:

    • Happiness: The Science Behind Your Smile, by Daniel Nettle

    • Authentic Happiness: Using the New Positive Psychology to Realize Your Potential for Lasting Fulfillment, by Martin E. P. Seligman

References

Addictive pleasure vs happiness

item.title: Blog: Pi Day
item.description:
Happy Pi day!

Today I realize I can still memorize 65 digits of π! That means I’m still as 65% smart as I was 18 years ago?! Awesome :)

Source

item.title: Blog: Song of the Day
item.description:
The brightest star in the night sky 夜空中最亮的星

Artist: Escape Plan

  • Style: Indie-rock
  • Language: Mandarin Chinese

Comment

I like this song because it

  • reflects the complexity of life and humanness, loss and hope, and love and dream.

  • always reminds me the endless warmth I receive from my family and friends :)

BTW, what is the brightest star in earth’s night sky?

Sirius, also known as the “Dog Star” or, more officially, Alpha Canis Majoris, for its position in the constellation Canis Major.

  • Sirius is a binary star dominated by a luminous main sequence star, Sirius A, with an apparent magnitude of -1.46.
  • Sirius A’s apparent brightness can be attributed both to its inherent luminosity, 20 times that of the Sun, and its proximity.
  • At just 8.7 light years away, Sirius is the seventh closest star to Earth.

Source

item.title: Blog: A Question
item.description: What are the things I started but not yet finished?

item.title: Blog: A Question
item.description:
What can I do well that I would love to do for an extended period of time?

Hal Jordan: “You can’t foresee all the consequences of your actions. But that’s no excuse to do nothing.”

item.title: Blog: Image Shape vs. Texture
item.description:
ImageNet-trained CNNs are biased towards texture; increasing shape bias improves accuracy and robustness

Robert Geirhos, University of Tubingen, ICLR 2019, GitHub Code

This new study showed the ImageNet data can induce texture bias in convolutional neural network (CNNs). However, adding a shape bias (use transformed Stylized-ImageNet as training data) can improve the accuracy and robustness.


Question 1:

If I know the image shape is the differentiating factor between 2 groups, should I nudge my model towards a shape bias?

1) Yes

  • Because machine learning model do use prior knowledge. Though there is a tradeoff, too many priors can limit the models capabilities to learn certain other things.

  • How can you integrate prior knowledge in deep learning?

    • Knowledge distillation

      • Hinton 2015, Distilling the Knowledge in a Neural Network

      • Aditya 2015, Visual Common sense for Scene Understanding Using Perception, Semantic Parsing and Reasoning

      • Aditya on Quora

    • Other

      • Paper Chakraborty 2017, Incorporation of prior knowledge in neural network model for continuous cooling of steel using genetic algorithm

      • Some discussions on StackOverflow

2) No

  • Neural networks have a very strong bias against including prior knowledge even when that prior knowledge is well known Marcus 2018

About bias

  • Human Bias

    • Interaction bias
    • Latent bias
    • Selection bias

  • How bias impacts training

  • Bias and Variance video by Andrew NG

High Bias High Variance
Train Big errors
Validation Big errors
Fitting Under Over
Recipe Bigger network, longer training time (select a NN architecture) More data, regularization (select a NN architecture)

* Predeep learning era: bias and variance tradeoff was often mentioned because of the lack of ability to train bigger network and getting more data.
  • Deep learning era: training a bigger network and getting more data often never hurts.

Question 2:

How to perpetrate human bias?
